attributes.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a 12-month history of low back pain, excess facial hair and increasing weight. She has no significant medical history and takes only oral contraceptive pills. She drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. On examination, she appears unwell with pulse rate 86/min, blood pressure 143/88 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her face is plethoric and round with acne. Hirsutism is evident mainly involving face and upper body. She has thin skin, ecchymoses, and wide purplish striae over her upper arms and abdomen.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This young woman presents with classic features of Cushing's syndrome including central weight gain, facial plethora, hirsutism, and characteristic skin changes (thin skin, easy bruising, and wide purplish striae). The combination of these clinical features warrants investigation for hypercortisolism.

24-hour urinary free cortisol is the most appropriate initial investigation. This test measures cortisol excretion over a full day, capturing both the normal morning peak and evening nadir, thus providing an integrated assessment of cortisol production. At least two collections are recommended to confirm the diagnosis, as single measurements can be affected by incomplete collection or stress.

Morning serum cortisol is not useful for diagnosing Cushing's syndrome because cortisol levels normally peak in the morning. There is substantial overlap between morning cortisol values in healthy individuals and those with Cushing's syndrome, making this test unable to distinguish between normal physiology and pathological hypercortisolism.

Plasma ACTH level is essential for determining the cause of Cushing's syndrome once hypercortisolism has been established. Low ACTH (<5 pg/mL) suggests an adrenal source, while elevated ACTH (>15 pg/mL) indicates either a pituitary or ectopic source. However, ACTH measurement should not precede confirmation of cortisol excess.

Inferior petrosal sinus sampling is an invasive procedure used to distinguish between pituitary and ectopic sources of ACTH in patients with ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome. A central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient greater than 2:1 at baseline or 3:1 after corticotropin-releasing hormone stimulation confirms a pituitary source. This test is performed only after establishing both hypercortisolism and ACTH dependence.

MRI brain is indicated when biochemical testing confirms ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome to identify a pituitary adenoma. However, up to 40% of corticotroph adenomas are not visible on MRI, and approximately 10% of the general population have incidental pituitary findings. Therefore, MRI should only be performed after appropriate biochemical workup, not as an initial investigation.

Other first-line screening tests for Cushing's syndrome include late-night salivary cortisol (which exploits the loss of normal circadian rhythm in Cushing's syndrome) and the 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (which tests for failure of normal feedback inhibition). The choice between these tests depends on patient factors and local availability.
learningPoints: The first-line screening tests for Cushing's syndrome are 24-hour urinary free cortisol (≥2 measurements), late-night salivary cortisol (≥2 measurements), and 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test.
Morning serum cortisol is not useful for diagnosing Cushing's syndrome due to significant overlap with normal values.
ACTH measurement differentiates ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent causes only after hypercortisolism is confirmed.
references: Libianto R, Yang J, Fuller PJ. Adrenal disease: An update. Aust J Gen Pract [Internet]. 2021 Jan–Feb;50(1–2):9–14. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/getattachment/82ae1bdd-9038-4957-934b-0c3a0ed61fbb/Adrenal-disease-An-update.aspx

Fleseriu M, Auchus R, Bancos I, et al. Consensus on Diagnosis and Management of Cushing’s Disease: A Guideline Update. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2021; 9: 847–75. Available from: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/landia/article/PIIS2213-8587(21)00235-7/fulltext

Dennis RJ, Conradie JH, Gillett MJ, Page MM. Detecting patients with Cushing's syndrome: The importance of initial test selection. Aust J Gen Pract. 2022 Jun;51(6):453-454. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-09-21-6178. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35637593/
